sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A developmental psychologist interviewed high-school seniors about career plans. Some students had explored multiple options and made a tentative commitment. Others had adopted a parent’s preferred plan without exploration, while another group felt overwhelmed and avoided thinking about the future. Students who had explored and committed described more confidence and less anxiety when discussing next steps. The psychologist used identity status theory to interpret the interviews.
questions.stem: Which group described the most confidence about next steps?

choices.C: Students who explored multiple options and made a tentative commitment
choices.A: Students who avoided thinking about the future
choices.B: Students who adopted a parent’s plan without exploration

explanations.C: Correct. Students who had both explored and committed reported more confidence and less anxiety when discussing future plans.
explanations.A: The passage does not support students who avoided thinking about the future; it points instead to students who explored multiple options and made a tentative commitment.
explanations.B: The passage does not support students who adopted a parent’s plan without exploration; it points instead to students who explored multiple options and made a tentative commitment.
explanations.D: The passage does not support all students reported identical confidence; it points instead to students who explored multiple options and made a tentative commitment.

questions.stem: Students who explored options and made a commitment best fit which identity status?

explanations.B: Correct. Identity achievement involves exploration followed by commitment, which matches the students who considered options and formed a plan.
explanations.A: The passage does not support foreclosure; it points instead to identity achievement.
explanations.C: The passage does not support diffusion; it points instead to identity achievement.
explanations.D: The passage does not support sensorimotor; it points instead to identity achievement.

questions.stem: Why should causal language be used cautiously for this passage?

choices.D: The design leaves room for alternative explanations or selection effects
choices.A: The passage contains no measurable variables
choices.B: All participant reports are automatically false
choices.C: The topic is unrelated to P/S content

explanations.D: Correct. The interviews show identity-status patterns but do not prove that status alone caused confidence, because confidence could also influence willingness to explore.
explanations.A: The passage includes concrete measures or observations, so this is incorrect.
explanations.B: Self-report can have limitations, but it is not automatically false.
explanations.C: The topic fits psychological, social, or behavioral science content.

questions.stem: Which application best follows from the passage?

choices.A: Help students explore options and make personally meaningful commitments
choices.B: Ignore the social or behavioral context described
choices.C: Use a one-size-fits-all intervention even when the passage shows different barriers
choices.D: Assume the measured pattern cannot be changed

explanations.A: Correct. Counseling that supports exploration and commitment follows from the group with greater confidence and lower anxiety.
explanations.B: Ignoring the described context would miss the mechanism emphasized in the passage.
explanations.C: A uniform intervention would not address the specific pattern described.
explanations.D: The passage supports practical response rather than fatalism.

choices.D: Identity status can help describe adolescent decision-making patterns without making them fixed or permanent
choices.A: The finding proves a universal law for every person
choices.B: The main construct is irrelevant because one limitation exists
choices.C: The passage supports only biological explanations

explanations.D: Correct. This conclusion fits identity theory while recognizing that statuses can change over time.
explanations.A: The passage supports a bounded interpretation, not a universal absolute.
explanations.B: A limitation weakens overclaiming but does not make the construct irrelevant.
explanations.C: P/S passages often involve social and psychological mechanisms beyond biology alone.

questions.passage: A transportation-safety researcher surveyed teenagers about risky driving. Teens who believed that most close friends texted while driving were more likely to report intending to do so themselves. Actual friend self-reports suggested that texting while driving was less common than many participants believed. The researcher concluded that misperceived descriptive norms may increase risky intentions and that correcting norm estimates could be a useful prevention strategy.
questions.stem: What belief was associated with greater intention to text while driving?

choices.C: The belief that most close friends texted while driving
choices.A: The belief that friends avoided all risk
choices.B: Accurate knowledge that texting was rare

explanations.C: Correct. Teens who believed close friends commonly texted while driving were more likely to intend the same risky behavior.
explanations.A: The passage does not support the belief that friends avoided all risk; it points instead to the belief that most close friends texted while driving.
explanations.B: The passage does not support accurate knowledge that texting was rare; it points instead to the belief that most close friends texted while driving.
explanations.D: The passage does not support having no beliefs about peers; it points instead to the belief that most close friends texted while driving.

questions.stem: The teens’ beliefs about how common the behavior was are examples of what?

explanations.B: Correct. Descriptive norms are perceptions of what others actually do. Believing friends text while driving is a perceived descriptive norm.
explanations.A: The passage does not support injunctive norms only; it points instead to descriptive norms.
explanations.C: The passage does not support operant punishment; it points instead to descriptive norms.
explanations.D: The passage does not support crystallized intelligence; it points instead to descriptive norms.

questions.stem: Why should causal language be used cautiously for this passage?

choices.A: The design leaves room for alternative explanations or selection effects
choices.B: The passage contains no measurable variables
choices.C: All participant reports are automatically false
choices.D: The topic is unrelated to P/S content

explanations.A: Correct. The survey measured perceived norms and intentions, not actual future driving behavior, so behavior tracking would strengthen inference.
explanations.B: The passage includes concrete measures or observations, so this is incorrect.
explanations.C: Self-report can have limitations, but it is not automatically false.
explanations.D: The topic fits psychological, social, or behavioral science content.

questions.stem: Which application best follows from the passage?

choices.C: Tell students that most peers do not text while driving
choices.A: Ignore the social or behavioral context described
choices.B: Use a one-size-fits-all intervention even when the passage shows different barriers
choices.D: Assume the measured pattern cannot be changed

explanations.C: Correct. Correcting the inflated descriptive norm should reduce the sense that risky driving is normal or expected.
explanations.A: Ignoring the described context would miss the mechanism emphasized in the passage.
explanations.B: A uniform intervention would not address the specific pattern described.
explanations.D: The passage supports practical response rather than fatalism.

choices.B: Misperceived peer norms may increase risky intentions by making a behavior seem more common than it is
choices.A: The finding proves a universal law for every person
choices.C: The main construct is irrelevant because one limitation exists
choices.D: The passage supports only biological explanations

explanations.B: Correct. This conclusion follows from the gap between perceived friend behavior and actual friend reports.
explanations.A: The passage supports a bounded interpretation, not a universal absolute.
explanations.C: A limitation weakens overclaiming but does not make the construct irrelevant.
explanations.D: P/S passages often involve social and psychological mechanisms beyond biology alone.
